tikz-graphtheory-dor1-28-5ebc50e3: Five small spanning trees side by side on n = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 vertices. Yellow circular nodes labelled A through E are connected by exactly n-1 edges in each, supporting the lemma that a tree on n vertices has n-1 edges.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-29-9a4a7d1f: Single yellow circular node labelled A drawn in isolation, illustrating the base case of the induction proof: a tree with one vertex and zero edges satisfies |E| = n - 1 = 0.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-30-5cc92d34: Pseudocode block titled 'Kruskal's Algorithm (Descriptive Version)'. Initialize T to empty; repeatedly pick the cheapest unused edge, skipping any edge that would create a cycle; stop once T contains |V|-1 edges, forming a minimum spanning tree of G.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-31-0fcfd924: Symmetric 10-by-10 distance table for ten Oregon cities (Ashland, Astoria, Bend, Corvallis, Crater Lake, Eugene, Newport, Portland, Salem, Seaside) with rotated headers. Cells give pairwise driving distances in miles, e.g. Astoria-Seaside=17, Crater Lake-Eugene=453.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-32-2302988f: Map-like graph of ten labelled Oregon cities partway through Kruskal's algorithm: edges Salem-Corvallis, Salem-Portland, Seaside-Astoria, Corvallis-Newport, Corvallis-Eugene, and Seaside-Portland are drawn; Ashland, Bend, and Crater Lake remain unconnected.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-33-f8408e8a: Map-like graph of ten Oregon cities showing the completed Kruskal minimum spanning tree with nine edges. Final additions Ashland-Crater Lake (108 mi), Bend-Eugene (128 mi), and Eugene-Ashland (178 mi) connect every city; total cable length 695 miles.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-34-13c6bb88: Undirected weighted graph with six yellow circular vertices A, B, C, E, F, G arranged hexagonally. Every pair is joined by an edge labelled with its integer weight (e.g. AB=11, BG=13, AE=14, EF=16, AC=33). Input graph for the Kruskal exercise.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-35-185deaa7: Python code listing 'Loading the graph into NetworkX' that builds the prior 6-vertex weighted graph in NetworkX: it imports nx, creates an undirected Graph, adds nodes A,B,C,E,F,G, and feeds the 15 weighted edges via G.add_weighted_edges_from(edges).
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-36-0fcfd924: Five-vertex graph with cities A, B, C, D, E as black dots, fully connected with dollar-cost edge labels (AB=4, AE=5, AC=8, DC=7, plus higher cross-edges). Edges AB, AE, AC, DC are thick red, marking the MST built by Prim's algorithm starting at A.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-37-110398b8: Same five-city phone-line graph (A, B, C, D, E) used to illustrate Prim's algorithm. Edges are labelled with dollar costs and the spanning tree edges AB, AE, AC, DC are drawn in thick red while the remaining inter-city edges are thin black.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-38-7c96767c: Boxed figure for Prim's algorithm: 7-vertex graph with vertices a, b, c, d, e, f, g as red circles. The minimum spanning tree edges d-a, d-f, a-b, b-e, e-c, e-g are drawn thick red; remaining edges are thin black with integer weights.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-39-c1f83ab1: Seven-vertex weighted graph for the shortest-path exercise. Vertices A, B, C, D, E, F, G in a roughly diamond layout with integer edge weights (AB=1, AC=4, BD=3, CD=2, DE=2, DF=4, EG=7, FG=6, plus chords). Goal: find the shortest path from A to G.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-40-b546b28e: Airfare table giving one-way dollar fares between Seattle, Honolulu, London, Moscow, and Cairo (e.g. Seattle-Honolulu $159, Honolulu-London $830, London-Moscow $245, Moscow-Cairo $329). Used as input for building a weighted graph.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-41-48dd4b39: Small undirected graph with five black-dot vertices arranged in two rows, with four edges (square plus a diagonal extension). One of three example graphs presented side-by-side asking 'Which of these graphs are connected?'.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-42-b2ad07b8: Small undirected graph with five black-dot vertices; edges form one triangle on the right plus a separate vertical segment on the left, leaving the graph in two components. One of three connectivity examples.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-43-b7833a77: Small undirected graph with five black-dot vertices fully linked through a series of edges so all vertices belong to one component. The third option in the 'Which of these graphs are connected?' exercise.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-44-e8e77b12: Eight-vertex graph drawn on a 3-by-3 grid layout with three short horizontal segments stacked at top, middle, and bottom; the components are not all linked, illustrating a disconnected graph for the second connectivity question.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-45-8e6c99fb: Eight-vertex graph on a 3-by-3 grid layout where a central vertex (1.5,1) has high degree, connecting most of the graph; one corner pair remains separated. Used for the second 'Which of these graphs are connected?' question.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-46-692351e0: Eight-vertex graph with edges forming a connected outer cycle plus several interior diagonals; the third option in the second connectivity exercise.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-47-5eb101ab: Weighted graph of a Eurail segment with seven nodes (Paris, Lyon, Bern, Frankfurt, Munchen, Amsterdam, Berlin). Edges are labelled with travel times in hours:minutes (e.g. Paris-Amsterdam 1:25, Lyon-Bern 3:50, Munchen-Berlin 5:45). Dijkstra exercise from Bern to Berlin.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-48-dafe33e7: Five-vertex graph with vertex (1,1) at the center linked to the four corners and a top-right corner; mixed odd and even degrees. One of three small graphs in the question 'Does each of these graphs have an Euler circuit?'.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-49-68959709: Six-vertex graph with several internal edges yielding several vertices of degree 3 or 4; presented as a candidate for an Euler circuit exercise.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-50-b5b22d83: Five-vertex graph featuring a multi-edge (two parallel edges drawn with bend left and bend right between the same pair of vertices) plus diagonal connections; given as a candidate Euler-circuit graph.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-51-60766773: Five-vertex graph forming a roughly pentagonal shape with three chords; used as a candidate Euler-circuit graph in the second 'Does each of these graphs have an Euler circuit?' exercise.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-52-c1e1499c: Six-vertex graph drawn as two stacked triangles sharing edges, presented as one of the candidate graphs in the second Euler-circuit exercise.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-53-02bb0b07: Five-vertex graph forming a square A-B-C-D with one interior vertex connected to two opposite corners; the third candidate in the second Euler-circuit exercise.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-54-ed818e71: Twelve-vertex 4-by-3 grid graph with horizontal, vertical, and a few diagonal edges; the exercise asks the student to eulerize the graph using as few edge duplications as possible and then find an Euler circuit.
